title: Triple Airway Manoeuvre (Airway Management)
content_markdown:
#### Indications

- Initial basic airway management.
- Any patient presenting with an obstructed airway, especially if the primary cause of obstruction is the tongue falling back.

#### Procedure

##### Head tilt

- Place one hand above the patient’s forehead.

- With your other hand hold the point of the jaw for support.

- Now gently extend the head by applying pressure above the forehead dependent upon the patient’s size and age (i.e. children and infants in the neutral position).

- Maintain this extension by applying jaw support.

- Look for spontaneous chest movement and listen for air movement.

##### Chin Lift / Jaw thrust

- Place both hands on each side of the patient’s head.

- Place your 3rd, 4th and 5th fingers around the angle of the mandible.

- Your index fingers should rest on the body of the mandible with your thumbs over the cheekbones.

- Lift the jaw by applying pressure to the angle of the mandible.

- The jaw will move at right angles to the line of the larynx.

- Look for spontaneous chest movement and listen for air movement.

#### Contraindications

- Spinal precautions.

#### Complications

- Head tilt in patients with suspected spinal injuries can increase risk of injury. Consider using jaw thrust.
- Hyperflexion or extension of a paediatrics neck may cause airway occlusion. Maintain neutral position as shown in **Figure 1**.

Figure 1
